query:
Why do I only breathe out of one nostril?
I was just sitting with my hand next to my nose and I realized that air was only coming out of the right nostril. Why is that? I would think I would use both, it seems much more efficient. Have I always only been breathing out of my right nostril?
llm_reasoning: The query is a series of related questions and observations about breathing through one nostril, but it does not have a clear logical structure with distinct parts that can be classified into the defined topologies. It is more of an inquiry seeking an explanation.

query:
Why can't our eyes smoothly transition from side to side without focusing on a moving object?
Why are we not able to slowly and smoothly look from side to side, or up and down in a single and smooth transition, given that we are able to do this if our eyes are focused on a moving object?
llm_reasoning: The query is asking about the same phenomenon in two different ways, but it is essentially a single continuous question about the inability of human eyes to smoothly transition without focusing on a moving object. There are no independent sub-queries, conditions, or mixed patterns.

query:
Why doesn't the cell membrane just...break apart?
Forgive me if this is a silly question. I can't understand the basics. Why doesn't the cell membrane just break apart? What's keeping the layers in the phospholipid bilayer together? I know that the membrane is embedded with proteins and lipids, but I still can't wrap my head around the "why". Are the hydrophobic interactions in the middle "stronger" than the hydrophilic interactions on the outside? What's keeping the individual phosphate heads together instead of, say, one of them just drifting away due to a nearby water molecule?
llm_reasoning: The query is asking for an explanation of a biological concept, specifically about the stability of the cell membrane. It does not have multiple distinct parts that can be answered independently, nor does it contain explicit conditions or a mix of independent and sequential/conditional parts. The question is seeking a continuous, detailed explanation of the factors contributing to the stability of the cell membrane.

query:
Why does an electrical shock freeze up muscles?
Why is it when someone comes into contact with an electrical supply that their body freezes up and is unable to move away from the electrical source?
Can someone explain this through a physiological perspective?
llm_reasoning: The query has multiple parts but they are all asking about the same phenomenon from different angles, seeking a single explanation. There is no branching or independent sub-queries, just a request for a detailed explanation of one concept.

query:
Below which temperature human muscles don't work?
When one exposes theirs body parts to cold environment (especially foot and hands), they may be numb, with temporarily blocked both muscles.
What is typical body temperature below which human muscles don't work properly?
Moreover, what is the mechanism behind it?
EDIT:
Interested in case when muscles almost cease to work (e.g one is not able to grasp a cup), not just in small perturbations to strength.
llm_reasoning: The query has multiple parts, but they are all related to the same main topic of how cold temperatures affect human muscles. The parts about the temperature threshold and the mechanism behind it form a single continuous reasoning path.

query:
If human eyes watch an area, what's the shape of its capturing shape? Rectangular? Half spherical?
A normal camera can capture a rectangular image. If human eyes watch an area, what's the shape of the captured region? Rectangular? Half-spherical?
llm_reasoning: The query is asking for a comparison between the shape of the captured region by a normal camera and human eyes, which can be answered in a single continuous reasoning path.

query:
Why do we have two of some organs, but not all?
We have two eyes, but we don't have two hearts. Why do humans have two of some organs, but not all?
llm_reasoning: The query is asking for an explanation of a biological phenomenon, which involves a single continuous reasoning path to explain the rationale behind having two of some organs and not others. There are no independent sub-queries, conditional logic, or mixed patterns.

query:
How are our senses dimmed during sleep?
Our senses are not as sensitive during sleep compared to wake. There is an arousal threshold during sleep that only when it is overcome will it wake a person up. My question is how is this arousal threshold lowered as sleep progresses? And how it is raised again during the latter stages of sleep?
llm_reasoning: The query has multiple parts, but they are all related to understanding the changes in sensitivity of senses during sleep and the concept of arousal threshold. The parts about how the arousal threshold is lowered as sleep progresses and how it is raised again are sequential and dependent on the initial context provided.

query:
Does taking a shower have the same effect on muscles as warming up?
When playing sports, coaches and trainers are always very clear that it is necessary to warm up and stretch. But sometimes athletes sometimes want to get an extra stretch in and don't want to go through the hassle of warming up.
Will taking a hot shower warm up muscles or does an athlete sctually have to do physical exercises like jogging in order to be ready to stretch?
I know this sounds like a question I could go on the physical fitness SE, but I'm really hoping for an answer involving thermodynamics and I doubt I'll get that there.
llm_reasoning: The query has multiple distinct parts, but they are not completely independent. The first part asks about the effect of taking a shower on muscles compared to warming up. The second part elaborates on the context and asks if a hot shower can warm up muscles or if physical exercises are necessary. These parts are related and build on each other, forming a single continuous reasoning path.

query:
Claim in article about why insects are attracted to light
In this article they are addressing the reason insects are attracted to light when they say
Heat radiation as an attractive component is refuted by the effect of LED lighting, which supplies negligible infrared radiation yet still entraps vast numbers of insects.
I don't see why attraction to LEDs shows they're not seeking heat. Could they for example be evolutionarily programmed to associate light with heat? So that even though they don't encounter heat near/on the LEDs they still "expect" to?
llm_reasoning: The query is a single continuous line of reasoning, questioning the article's claim and proposing an alternative explanation. There are no distinct independent parts, no conditional logic, and no mixed patterns.
